item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd be down for that — the image is certainly large enough. I don't know how that code works, though (but I'm sure I can look it up!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you thought about making your images expandable so we can read the box art?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love me some Seventh Door, I think I saw a copy at a flea market once but the vendor wouldn’t sell it to me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great stuff from Hippo as always. Malevolent old-school video games have pushed my buttons just right ever since I first stumbled upon the Creepypasta wiki as a kid, and this article captured the unique sense of horror they present extremely well. Solid work all around.

item.title: Re: this is my sonic.EXE
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image of the mansion used in this article is also from here, and is licensed under CC0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sadly I read this for the first time on mobile, meaning I couldn't make out the images, so all of that intricacy was lost on me.

One nitpick

several fingernails torn from Mr. Rogers' left hand

If it is just fingernails, how is anyone able to tell they are from his left hand?

+1, for not skimming, additionally I liked it alot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Delicious, finally some good fucking video game SCPs

I love the technical aspect to this. How often is an article able to go into the nitty-gritty about how something malfunctions to get around normal reality? It's all perfectly believable, and even delves into some creepypasta standbys without being standard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved the use of an obscure piece of gaming history in the 10NES lock-out chip. +1

Normally I hate SCPs that are 'things that kill you', but this is really working for me.

Maybe it's how low-grade the creepiness is compared to a lot of SCPs, maybe it's that last image (I hadn't noticed the Blue Wraith in other parts), maybe it's how well thought out this is. Whatever reason I love this and it is one of the few SCPs to actually scare me.

Updooted with extreme prejudice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
300 cartridges have been made. 25 are missing with their players. An unknown number are broken and cannot be played at all.

I don't know why, but I have this curiosity to see what would happen if all the remaining cartridges are acquired and an arbitrary number of D-Class were used to play the game with the Game Genie to cheat and get the seventh key.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the slow creep of the horror. The fact that as a fan of weird old games my brain really wants to know what the ending would be like and how much I wanted more screen shots, just adds to my enjoyment of this skip.

I also enjoy that the foundation misses the most obvious way of keeping people from seeking it out; posting a fake rom online with the ending being really dumb like you open the seventh door and get the old bland a winner is you screen or oh no you really just unleashed the evil. I don't know why, it just feels like a nice "they're missing something " even if you didn't intend it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+
-

The seventh level does not contain the seventh key.

SCP-2747 is fucking amazing. If you can put it in something unique and interesting in its own right, which you seem to be very good at, take all my upvotes!

The prisoners use the protagonist's sprite rather than their own.

Part way through his run, the video abruptly stopped. A follow-up investigation determined that Mr. Rogers had gone missing; authorities suspected foul-play after discovering several fingernails torn from Mr. Rogers' left hand inside his NES console.

I can see why the Foundation wants everyone to think it's an urban legend. This is the stuff of creepypastas.

*checks files*

Total files: 7

terriblefate.gif

Glorious.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
reading the article
"It seems that the only anomaly is that it has more memory than normal. This should just be in the anomalous objects log."

To date, SCP-4054 has been linked to 25 disappearances. It remains under investigation.

OH

+ show block
– hide block

Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate this immensely! It's all very interesting, and — particularly given my interest in making this article 'feel' as real as possible — very useful. I intend to go through the article and make a number of changes integrating aspects of what you've pointed out here. Thank you for making this post!

Aside: Large posts like this break for mobile users; if possible, could you edit your post to put the majority of it in a collapsible? Like this: [[collapsible]] <text> [[/collapsible]].

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really nice entry! I especially liked the implication that the reader can perceive things in the screenshots that the Foundation researchers cannot.

I have some technical suggestions that are probably mostly only of concern to other computer nerds like me. Don't worry about these too much, I think your entry is just fine as it is! But maybe you'll find these notes interesting anyways:

+ show block
– hide block
  • The entry describes "extracting SCP-4054's source-code", however the source code to the game is not found on the cartridge at all. Source code is used during development, but is not part of a released NES game. You could instead refer to "extracting the contents of SCP-4054's ROM", or if you wanted to be really specific, extracting its "PRG ROM", which hold a game's executable machine code and associated data (including level data).
  • The entry refers to SRAM, with an implication that corrupting it would reduce the functionality of the game. However, NES cartridge SRAM is only used for battery-backed saved game storage, so damaging it would probably only cause issues with loading and saving, not general gameplay. Then again, it's not impossible for disparate parts of the cartridge to interfere with each other when damaged…
  • The entry implies that the cartridge contains an effectively infinite amount of level data. However, this is more complicated than "merely" having a supernaturally large PRG ROM chip! An NES cartridge does not provide data as a stream, like a download. Instead, the NES console (or cartridge dumping device) requests numbered sections (called "banks") of the cartridge's ROM, one-at-a-time as needed. The bank numbers normally don't go very high, which sets a practical limit on usable NES cartridge space, regardless of the cartridge's physical capacity.

Some more detail about that last point. Each ROM bank is at most 32kb in size, because the NES processor can't address any more than that. So a seemingly infinite NES cartridge would have to have a seemingly infinite number of ROM banks. This aligns with the story's premise, because every NES cartridge has its own "mapper", responsible for (among other things) handling requests for ROM banks. There are a lot of different mapper designs out there: some, like Nintendo's MMC3 mapper, are in dozens of different games, while others are custom made for only one particular game.

So, let's say The Seventh Door has a unique mapper that provides a seemingly infinite number of memory banks. We have to assume that this mapper behaves in a really unusual way, because no contemporary NES mapper allowed requesting from more than 128 banks (for a total size of 4 megabytes). A mapper that somehow allows requesting banks beyond that would present difficulties for off-the-shelf NES cartridge dumping hardware, because such hardware has to know how a cartridge's mapper accepts requests before it can read the cartridge. So we have to assume that the researcher went through the trouble of reverse-engineering the game far enough to understand its novel method interacting with the mapper, and then customizing their cartridge reader to incorporate their findings.

Maybe the mapper accepts a sequence of commands to select a bank, rather than a single command. This would allow for a much greater number of addressable banks than normal. Or maybe the mapper abandons the usual numbered way of addressing banks entirely, and instead accepts "next bank" and "previous bank" commands, which could be repeated any number of times. That would make the cartridge behave a little more like a streaming download, and you would not be able to predict (or jump straight to) the final block. To the best of my knowledge, no real NES cartridge mappers did either of these things, but there's no rule saying they couldn't!


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Anyways, nothing in the text of the entry precludes any of this, so I wouldn't worry about correcting anything for it. However, I thought you might find it interesting, since the researcher would have had to go through quite some trouble to begin even investigating the cartridge's unusually high capacity!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Balancing subtle versus overt tends to be my thing; a lot of my articles are exercises in seeing how much I can accomplish with scarcity and insinuation. That being said, the Hand of Glory is a fair point, and something that should have likely been explained (expecting readers to know its actual history is a bit much). I'll add a footnote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First time reading: Wait, why is this even anomalous?

Second time reading, specifically looking for anomalies: Oh, okay, it's way more data than an NES should have, and also some streamer disappeared while playing. I still feel like I'm missing something.

Third time reading, after viewing comments: Oh FUCK.

While I absolutely love the subtle atmosphere you've created, I feel like it's maybe just a little too subtle. Again, I would absolutely never have figured out what was going on if I hadn't read your spoiler comment earlier in the thread. Maybe a few things could be done to make it a tad more obvious; the Wraith blends in with the background and is hard to spot in the screenshots, to the point that I didn't spot it until I saw your comment and specifically started looking for it. Also, this might be just me, but I've never heard of a "Hand of Glory" before, so a footnote defining it for the audience might be useful. Novote for now, but I look forward to seeing your future projects!

item.title: To Translators:
item.content:encoded:
One more final note: I've noticed a couple of translators have posted translations of this article (which is super cool!). I've also just realized that the back of the box-art contains relevant information for the article, but since it's an image, it's really hard to do anything with that. You could just leave it as is, or provide a translation of the text somewhere — but for more enterprising translators, I figured I'd provide the GIMP *.XCF file here.

Feel free to delete the text-layer, replace it with your translation, then export as a *.PNG — and 'paste' this *.PNG over the pre-existing box-art's back-panel. Or — don't! This is a lot of work to go through for just a translation of a goofy NES creepypasta, but I thought I'd provide the option for anyone who wants to do it. ETA: Also, keep in mind — if you add this text, I went with black text, then used 'Brightness-Contrast' (under the Colors menu) to wash the text out by decreasing its contrast (so it 'looks' like part of the box).

(And as always, thanks to all the translators for all the work they do!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article oozes quality. Honestly, I do feel it's a bit… squandered on this slowburn of a creepypasta, but the production values and and Hippo-trademarked tightness win me over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Between this, the Eskimo one, and Cimm's rpgmaker game this website is amassing a wonderful collection of well done game skips. Honestly I think this might be my favorite of the ones you've made this month, though I can't put my finger on why. Either way, I feel more than ever that you're one of the best authors to ever grace this site Hippo- the true successor and evolution to the Gears style of lean technical horror.

item.title: Re: this is my sonic.EXE
item.content:encoded: Posting to note I've changed the box-art image to be compliant with CC (thanks to C-Dives who pointed out I made a mistake with the art I used). The links have been updated accordingly; the new image uses this, which is public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! And yeah; part of my concern with this is that the article itself isn't about the anomaly (which is that the game contains far more content than it possibly should), but about all the things that operate adjacent to the anomaly.

The containment procedures are already really, really long; I feel like I'm stretching the reader's attention with them as it is. That being said, there's one bit in the list of game features I can modify to imply an even deeper anomaly:

  • To date, researchers have uncovered several hundred levels beyond the seventh (described in the manual as the final level, and the location of the seventh key). These stages consist of vast networks of tunnels, crypts, abandoned factories, and empty underground cities. The precise number of additional levels beyond the seventh has not yet been determined, but vastly exceeds the storage capacity of the cartridge's ROM chip.

This might help emphasize that the anomaly here is that the game is fucking huge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh, thanks — you're right; I thought this was just a CC 2.0 license, but it's non-commercial use. When I get home, I'll modify the image with a fully CC compliant painting of a spooky house. It shouldn't be that hard; I still have the GIMP files I used to produce the box-art, and the house painting is just one of the layers (so I should be able to swap it out with another one with relatively little work).

Thank you for pointing this out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jeez, man. I've always been a fan of how the horror in your articles is vague at first and the way you gradually ramp it up while leaving the reader to figure things out, but this takes it to a whole new level. I couldn't figure out anything about it at first, but then I went back through and started to notice more details each time. Awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One big problem; the box art contains Non-Commercial license image (haunted house illustration), which is not available on SCP wiki. I strongly recommend that you fix current image — if the box art gets omitted, your above-mentioned spoilers would not work well. Moreover, this art is too good to be entirely thrown away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Video game spookyspagetti done right, with no hyper-realistic eyes to be found. My only real complaint is that the only thing making it seem anomalous is the final addendum. You have that bit about it having more ROM than an NES cartridge should, but that seems pretty minor. You could foreshadow it by putting something in the containment procedures about not modifying the game to reach the later levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oops; thanks — fixed!

And sorry to hear that it didn't work for you — this is and SCP-3561 are experiments for me; I want to see if I can accomplish horror by eschewing wham-lines and focusing on atmosphere and restraint.

I'll eventually break down what's going on here, but a few notes (on the off-chance you're curious):

Spoilers
– hide block
  • The Foundation says there are no monsters present in the game. And yet a monster appears in every screenshot.
  • The monster's identity is stated in the box art; the Blue Wraith. You can see it on the cover (its by the house's entrance) and again on the back of the box-art (the blue figure in the bottom left demo-screenshot).
  • The player dies randomly if they stay still too long; the Foundation doesn't know why. Again, this is to emphasize that the Foundation is missing something.
  • A Hand of Glory is an occult artifact consisting of the hand of a murderer who was hanged used as a candle; when lit, any who see it are incapable of approaching you. inky49's fingernails were torn from his left hand.
  • There are 25 disappearances. There are 25 figures (other than the Blue Wraith) in the final screenshot.
  • Also, just a silly easter egg: The HiveSoft logo is HiveFind, from bzzip.exe.

I expect none of this will make the skip 'more' enjoyable for you; if I have to explain it, then it didn't work. I'm just throwing this out there to show that I'm not picking spooky elements for the sake of spookiness — there's a story I'm trying to insinuate, here.

(As an aside: That's the reason I love Killswitch so much. Most video game creepypastas include elements purely for spookiness' sake; things don't happen for a reason, they happen to creep you out. Killswitch feels like it isn't trying to scare you, and that's what makes it interesting. I wanted to evoke that same sense with this article and the other one I posted, An Unfinished Work.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The user is 'ink49' in the image caption but 'inky49' in the text.

I don't get this one, it reads just like the aforementioned spooky video game creepypastas. What story may be contained in the images is difficult for me to figure out and also not as visually enjoyable, both as a result of the image quality of retro video games vs. paintings as in the preceding hippo SCP.

item.title: Re: Minor typo
item.content:encoded: Oops; thanks! Fixed. ~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ on a candlestick Hippo, how many good SCPs are you gonna release this month?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I didn’t quite see that as I really skimmed that section. Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mentioning Strangled Red and Godzilla NES really gave me nostalgia there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The contents of each cartridge vastly exceeds the storage limitations of its ROM2 chip. Extracting SCP-4054's source-code produces a data-file too large to be contained on any currently available device.

Right there in like the second paragraph, but go off I guess

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: several

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I just don’t get it, but what exactly makes this game anomalous. It’s just an unfinished 8-bit game that’s slightly unsettling in its design and gameplay deviations and is involved in a few disappearances. This stuff is all well and freaky, but it doesn’t actually seem anomalous at all.

Novote, because it’s well written but not really fitting of an SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of a much subtler versions of those video game creepypastas like NES Godzilla and Strangled Red. This is certainly unnerving in all the right places, although you may have lost me a bit when you started to explain about the cartridge's effects on the system which I have no clue what it does other than it messes it up. The parts that explained it in a technical aspect lessened the whole experience for me but it was still enough to creep me out.

Wait a second.

A follow-up investigation determined that Mr. Rogers had gone missing; authorities suspected foul-play after discovering several fingernails torn from Mr. Rogers' left hand inside his NES console.

How can you have seven fingernails in your hand?

item.title: Minor typo
item.content:encoded:
The Seventh Door was shipped prior completion.

Probably should be "The Seventh Door was shipped prior to completion.

Good story, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the numerology of the article is too close to SCP-2747 and the monster uncannily similar to SCP-3117, I really like how you use the images to convey the story rather than the text. It ends up reminding me of Stephen King’s The Sun Dog, and I actually did get some frisson from seeing the Blue Wraith get closer and closer to its target.

item.title: this is my sonic.EXE
item.content:encoded:
Oh, dear...
 

You've met with a terrible fate, haven't you?

SOURCES:

  • Images Link, Link, Link, Link, Link, Link, Link, and — finally — Link.
  • The Hivesoft Logo was made by my house-mate for this SCP.
  • Inspired by the old Killswitch creepypasta.

FEEDBACK:

  • minmin deserves credit here for helping me brainstorm the idea and figure out the color-scheme.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
